title: Expansion on a Personal and Professional Level: Erin's Daily Fundamentals System
summary: Erin walked participants through her 20-year daily logging system called 'Erin's Daily Fundamentals in Action,' covering five core categories: energy, work/service, love and family, non-negotiables, and top five wins. The session emphasized that personal and professional growth must run at the same pace and that daily reflection and documentation accelerates growth and self-belief. The group then opened for Q&A covering morning routines, vacation strategies, delegating to teams, book recommendations, and business milestones.

teachings.explanation: Erin separates her daily activities into 'rabbit holes' (the detailed, daily actions she dives into) and 'galaxy goals' (the big, universal objectives those actions serve). The framework keeps daily work connected to long-term vision and ensures focus drives results rather than wishes.
teachings.quote: I separate my daily, my dailies into my rabbit holes, like the rabbit holes of details. I will go down these rabbit holes every day. And on the right side, I call them galaxy goals, which are why I go down rabbit holes.

teachings.concept: You Get What You Focus On, Not What You Want
teachings.explanation: Wanting something is not enough — sustained, deliberate focus is what produces results. Erin uses this as a daily reminder at the top of her tracking sheet to keep herself honest about where her attention is going.
teachings.quote: You get what you focus on, not what you want. We all want a bunch of things, but unless we focus on it, we're not going to get it.

teachings.explanation: Inspired by a 44-year sober AA member, this principle means committing to your non-negotiables unconditionally — not when you feel like it, but regardless of circumstances. It creates clarity and removes the daily decision of whether or not to follow through.
teachings.quote: He said, this is the way I live my life. I do X regardless. Regardless was the word he used. And it stops there. That is a very clear way to live.

teachings.concept: Always Value Others AND Be Valuable to Others
teachings.explanation: Two distinct rules govern Erin's work and service category. The first is to remember to value the people supporting you even when frustrated. The second is to actively be the person who adds value every time — in work, in conversation, and with family.
teachings.quote: Always value others... Second rule is be valuable to others. Be that person who is valuable to others. Every time you talk to them, every time you work with them, every time you're with your kids, be valuable.

teachings.concept: Be World Class, Not Just the Best
teachings.explanation: Erin challenges participants to think beyond being 'the best' in their market — a relative standard — and instead aim for world-class performance, which is an absolute, universal standard tied to galaxy-level thinking.
teachings.quote: Be world class at whatever you're doing. Not just the best. What's the best? The best doesn't mean anything. But be world class.

teachings.explanation: Recording five wins each day serves as an antidote to the tendency to overlook personal growth and positive impact. Over time, these logs build genuine self-belief and provide evidence of progress during difficult periods.
teachings.quote: I am a big believer in documenting wins every day. Because we take for granted, and oftentimes we may not recognize, how we did add value to someone's life.

teachings.explanation: By consistently logging daily events and outcomes, patterns emerge across clients, projects, and relationships. Recognizing these patterns enables better anticipation and proactive delivery of the experiences others want.
teachings.quote: When things happen, recognize the patterns. Because as you really look at your business and your family, patterns develop. And the faster you can learn these patterns, the more you can anticipate correctly.

teachings.explanation: Avoiding financial review keeps you in a protective, reactive mindset. Reviewing projections, revenue, payroll, and expenses daily normalizes the discomfort and enables better strategic decisions about hiring, projects, and growth.
teachings.quote: If we face this every day, then we're more comfortable looking at the big picture rather than staying in our own little world trying to protect it.

teachings.concept: Scorecards for Hiring and Team Clarity
teachings.explanation: Sharing a performance scorecard during the interview process sets expectations upfront and self-selects for candidates who are aligned with the standards required. The problem is often the leader not being clear, not the team underperforming.
teachings.quote: The problem was me not being clear. Now that I'm clear, they know what to do. As soon as people got more clear on like, this is what's expected, these things have to happen — and as soon as we did that, the problem was me.

actionable_advice: Create your own daily fundamentals sheet with categories for energy, work/service, love and family, non-negotiables, and wins — and fill it out every single day.
Identify your 'galaxy goals' (big life and business objectives) and connect your daily rabbit hole activities directly to those goals.
Create three opportunities per day through conversations with people — calls to architects, designers, or clients — and do it regardless of how you feel.
Capture two insights every day from ordinary observations in your environment to train your mind to notice patterns and learning opportunities.
Identify at least one person each day whose life you made better through something you said, did, or covered for them, and log it.
Look at your financial projections, revenue, monthly payroll, and expenses daily so you can make proactive decisions about hiring, projects, and cash flow.
Write down your top five wins at the end of each day — including personal and professional — and keep a running journal to reference during difficult periods.
Review your goals three times a day (morning, afternoon, and before bed) for just a few seconds each time to keep them front of mind without over-analyzing.
Use scorecards for project managers, architects, designers, and trades — and share them during the interview process so candidates self-select based on alignment.
Treat your energy routine (exercise, recovery, mindfulness) as non-negotiable infrastructure for the day, adjusting the timing based on your season of life.
client_challenges: Balancing the demands of building a business with being present for a young family, especially with limited sleep and no support system.
Feeling like entrepreneurial success looks easy for everyone else on social media while personally struggling with the grind of building a business.
Difficulty knowing how to set a course or develop vision early in a career when growing up without strong guidance or role models.
Figuring out when and how to take vacations or step away when the business feels dependent on the owner's constant presence.
Transitioning from being deep in operational details to trusting a team to run the engine while figuring out where the owner's focus should shift.
Managing outside opinions and unsolicited advice from friends and family when making bold business decisions like taking on a spec remodel project.
Staying in startup mode for too long and not knowing how to accelerate through business phases toward more freedom and delegation.
notable_quotes: On staying stagnant: 'It occurred to me — he is in the same place now that he was 20 years ago. Same place. So the light bulb went off like, whoa, what are we going to do about this?'
On daily habits: 'This is 20 years worth of stuff, but it's like programmed now. It's a matter of just programming how you're going to think during the day.'
On self-belief: 'Imagine if you don't analyze how you've proven yourself right over the years, how can you believe in yourself? And that'll stop you dead in your tracks.'
On motivation to wake up early: 'I wanted to wear tennis shoes. And I'm not kidding you. That was my goal. No joke. I wanted to wear tennis shoes. And I was like, ooh, building fits.'

On not being a natural morning person: 'I don't wake up happy. Literally, I have to do all of this stuff and learn in the morning to then be able to be a happy person during the day. That's just what is required to show up.'

On owning problems: 'We own half the problem. Called the designer, said hey, we need to pay for half of this. I don't want to stick you with the bill. And that's what happens when you just do the right things.'
